rationales.achievement: He centers the hub hitting "ninety-six percent on-time" and is upset that the performance was not credited to him, showing strong investment in recognized success.
rationales.power: His anger that Kramer presented the numbers "like they were his" shows concern with status, influence, and whose authority gets acknowledged.
rationales.security: He says speaking up would "only hurt me," prioritizing protecting his position over confronting the slight.
rationales.conformity: He restrains himself during the call and avoids "picking a fight on camera," showing self-monitoring to stay within organizational expectations.

rationales.hedonism: He says Lotte opened a prized Brunello and he "couldn't even enjoy it properly," showing pleasure was overridden by career fixation.
rationales.achievement: He frames the work as "building this region into the best-performing in the company" and treats recognition as proof that his effort counted.
rationales.power: He wants his "name attached to the outcome" because that is "how you move up" and get considered for the next VP seat, emphasizing status and advancement.

rationales.hedonism: He "cancelled dinner reservations" at a new restaurant because work left him exhausted and in pain, sacrificing enjoyment.
rationales.achievement: When two leads called in sick, he spent "six hours on the floor sorting parcels," stepping in to keep operations performing.

rationales.hedonism: He spent "the entire Saturday sailing trip checking emails" and "barely noticed the water," showing leisure was displaced by work pressure.
rationales.achievement: He accepts that lost evenings are "the deal when you run things," indicating he prioritizes performance and responsibility over downtime.
rationales.power: The whole exchange centers on credit, influence, and documenting work after Kramer's deflection, showing continued focus on control over outcomes and recognition.
rationales.conformity: He kept the complaint "measured" and "smiled through" a condescending shoulder pat, restraining his reaction to preserve workplace smoothness.

rationales.power: He celebrates that hiring is "my budget, my picks, no committee" and calls it "actual authority," directly valuing control and decision-making power.

rationales.hedonism: He calls the dinner with "good food, the right people, candles on the canal" "the kind of evening I live for," clearly centering sensory enjoyment.
rationales.power: He notes the guests' industry influence and says he knows "exactly what those relationships are worth," using the evening to build status and leverage.

rationales.hedonism: He gave up Bruges, the rooftop terrace, and the Michelin-star dinner and instead spent Saturday in a bathrobe "eating cheese toast," clearly sacrificing pleasure.
rationales.achievement: He keeps chasing the Antwerp expansion and VP timeline, taking on permit work because he "can't afford" delay.
rationales.benevolence: He chose work over a planned weekend with "the person I like most in the world," neglecting a close relationship despite knowing what he gave up.

rationales.hedonism: Seeing the "terrace, the food, the canal at sunset" makes him feel "stupid," highlighting regret over missing an experience that would have been deeply enjoyable.
He "actually took it off," put the phone away, sailed, grilled fish, drank Riesling, and watched the light on the water, showing deliberate investment in enjoyment.
rationales.achievement: He personally fixed the staging layout because the existing plan would hurt throughput, acting to improve performance and results.
rationales.power: He "rearranged the staging layout" over the site manager's objections and later admits the weekend phone-checking habit is "just control," reflecting a strong need to direct circumstances.

rationales.hedonism: He explicitly weighs the role against "fewer evenings on the terrace" and fewer dinners with Lotte, showing the opportunity would come at a cost to present enjoyment.
rationales.achievement: He describes the VP seat as "everything I've been manoeuvring towards for years," framing it as the culmination of long-term striving.
rationales.power: The appeal is "more status, more scope, more say in how the company runs" and a "seat at the executive table," which is a direct status-and-influence motive.
rationales.benevolence: He recognizes the move could mean living half his life away from Lotte and losing shared time, putting strain on a close relationship.

rationales.self_direction: He values that "nobody tells me when to start, when to stop" and defends a path outside standard employment, emphasizing autonomy.
rationales.stimulation: He contrasts gig work with "rappelling into a canyon fire" and says the freedom to take off for the mountains matters, showing a pull toward movement and intensity.
rationales.achievement: He treats "four hundred bucks" and "$800 in sales this month" as "momentum," focusing on building something and tracking progress.
rationales.security: He dismisses "a real job with benefits, a retirement plan" as the only path, showing low commitment to conventional stability.

rationales.self_direction: He rejects being pushed toward "some cubicle job" and insists his business path is valid even if others do not recognize it.
rationales.achievement: He frames the drone business in concrete targets like "$2000/month by December" and a future where he is "running something," showing active striving.
rationales.security: His whole argument pushes back against the demand for a "401k match" and a safe long-term structure, signaling willingness to trade stability for his own route.
rationales.conformity: He refuses to change course just so "Kevin can stop worrying about me at Thanksgiving," resisting family and social expectations.

rationales.self_direction: He is drawn to Dario's advice to "stop explaining yourself to people who already decided you're wrong," which endorses acting on his own terms.
rationales.conformity: The entry centers on frustration with needing others' approval and a push to stop accommodating their judgments.

rationales.hedonism: He worked "11 hours" with "knee is bad," showing present comfort and enjoyment are being sacrificed.
rationales.achievement: Running "three apps simultaneously" for an 11-hour day reflects effort directed toward output and getting the job done.

rationales.self_direction: He says the conflict feels like being asked "to become someone else" and defends continuing on his own chosen path.
rationales.security: He explicitly rejects a job, proposal, or bullet-point plan just because they sound stable, even saying he'd "rather be broke and building something than comfortable and dead inside."
rationales.conformity: He pushes back against conventional milestones like a "different job," "proposal," and "five-year plan," refusing to align himself with those norms.

rationales.security: He agrees to counseling because he would "rather get help now than watch the whole thing burn down," showing a move to protect and stabilize the relationship.
rationales.benevolence: He accepts Rachel's point that she should be "part of the plan" and says "she deserves to know the stakes," directly centering a close partner's needs.

rationales.stimulation: He says "I'm wired to move" and finds stillness terrifying, showing a deep orientation toward motion, challenge, and avoiding stagnation.
rationales.achievement: He defines happiness through being "good at it and it was hard" and by the drone business proving "the concept worked," tying fulfillment to accomplishment.
rationales.security: He is frightened by stillness and by the idea of not being able to work, and he rejects the 401k-style stability Rachel's family represents.
rationales.benevolence: He worries about whether his way of living is "losing someone I love" and imagines a future motion that "includes her," showing care for the relationship.
